title: SEGMENT 14 — The Boston-Tübingen big screen: scaling Heidelberg to a vertebrate

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The Boston-Tübingen big screen: scaling Heidelberg to a vertebrate
In December 1996, a special issue of the journal Development appeared, devoted to the results of the Boston-Tübingen zebrafish screen.

Two large consortia of fish geneticists — one led by Wolfgang Driever at Boston (then at MGH, later Freiburg), and one led by Christiane Nüsslein-Volhard at Tübingen, Germany — had carried out the largest systematic mutagenesis screen ever attempted in a vertebrate.
The plan: mutagenize zebrafish, screen tens of thousands of embryos for any visible developmental defect, and identify the genes required for each step of vertebrate development.
Nüsslein-Volhard, you'll notice, was one of the leaders.
The Heidelberg screen in flies had been her work.
Now, sixteen years later, she was doing the same thing in fish — taking the saturation-mutagenesis strategy that worked in fly and applying it to the simplest vertebrate she could find.
The intellectual lineage is direct: Heidelberg in flies in 1980 became Boston-Tübingen in fish in 1996.
The scale was enormous.
The two consortia together processed over a million embryos.

They identified about 4,000 mutants with visible developmental defects, falling into hundreds of complementation groups — meaning hundreds of distinct genes required for vertebrate embryonic development.

The mutants were organized by phenotype: defects in body axis, defects in somite formation, defects in heart development, defects in brain patterning, defects in eye development, defects in pigmentation, and on and on.
Some highlights.

The screen identified mutants in essentially every major signaling pathway: Sonic hedgehog mutants (which gave embryos with cyclopia, a single fused eye); Bmp pathway mutants (with dorsoventral patterning defects); Wnt pathway mutants; FGF pathway mutants.
The pathways were the same ones identified in flies, mice, and other systems, but the fish made it possible to identify them all systematically, in a single coordinated screen, over a few years.
The mutants gave names to the field: one-eyed pinhead, cyclops, no tail, floating head, silberblick, trilobite, knypek.
(Notice the Drosophila-style poetic naming carried over into fish.)
Each one is now a well-characterized gene, often with a direct human homolog implicated in a developmental disorder.
one-eyed pinhead, for instance, encodes a co-receptor for Nodal signaling, and Nodal pathway mutations in humans cause heterotaxy syndromes — defects in the left-right asymmetry of internal organs.
The screen, like Heidelberg before it, demonstrated that saturation mutagenesis works in vertebrates.
Until 1996, many people thought vertebrate development was too complex, too redundant, too distributed across cell types to be amenable to single-gene forward genetics in the way fly development was.
Boston-Tübingen disproved that.
Vertebrate development could be dissected gene by gene.
The fish was the right vertebrate to do it in.
The Boston-Tübingen screen is one of the great achievements of model-organism biology and a landmark for the strategy of scaling up genetic tools.
It established the gene catalogue for vertebrate development that subsequent molecular work has been mapping in detail for two decades.
The big idea: the Boston-Tübingen screen scaled the Heidelberg strategy to a vertebrate and showed that the systematic genetics that worked in fly also worked in fish.
Plus, by extension, in any vertebrate.
The patterning genes of zebrafish embryos are largely the same genes that pattern mouse, human, and chick embryos.
The catalogue, once built, generalizes.
